turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument next in Case 13ﾂｭ7211, Jennings v. Stephens. Mr. Schaffer.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. Ninety years ago, this Court held in American Railway that an appellee need not crossﾂｭappeal to raise an argument in support of the judgment that does not seek to enlarge his rights. Petitioner, who prevailed on an ineffective assistance of counsel claim in the district court, did not have to crossﾂｭappeal here for two reasons. First, he did not seek more relief than the new punishment hearing granted to him in this ﾂｭﾂｭ in the judgment. Second, he raised an argument that the district court rejected as an alternate basis to affirm the judgment, and if he did not need to crossﾂｭappeal, he did not need a COA. We made ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. He ﾂｭﾂｭ I don't know if this is a new argument or a new issue. And the reason I raise that is when the judgment issued here by the district court, it specified two errors that had occurred and basically told the State court, fix those two or you have to release the defendant. Let's assume they fixed those two, and then could you come back and say release him anyway because they didn't fix the third that I lost on?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: No, not under those circumstances.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So how is it not more relief?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: How is it not more relief or different relief?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Because the single error is that he was denied the effective assistance of counsel. That is a single claim, and it was based on three allegations of deficient performance.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Well, now, wait a minute. I ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, I have ﾂｭﾂｭ we ﾂｭﾂｭ we don't evaluate whether you had good counsel or bad counsel. You may have Clarence Darrow and still be denied effective assistance of counsel if Clarence Darrow makes one mistake. I mean, when ﾂｭﾂｭ when we find that there has been ineffective assistance of counsel, I think that means counsel failed to do one thing that he should have done. But there's ﾂｭﾂｭ there's no such general finding that counsel was ﾂｭﾂｭ was, in gross, ineffective. You're ﾂｭﾂｭ you're ﾂｭﾂｭ you're describing it as though ﾂｭﾂｭ as though that's what the finding is. That ﾂｭﾂｭ that's not what we hold when we find ineffective assistance of counsel. We find that this particular counsel made this mistake. That's it.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, let's take that a step further. What the Court does to analyze an IAC claim is to first isolate the errors of counsel. It could be one; it could be a hundred. It doesn't matter. You accumulate them and consider them together to determine prejudice. If the deficiencies in performance constitute sufficient prejudice to undermine confidence in the verdict, then counsel was constitutionally ineffective, not because of A, B, C, or D, but because the totality of his representation did not meet the constitutional standard. So it's not a matter of fixing any particular error of counsel. And that's where I believe the ﾂｭﾂｭ the State's position is a little awry because a Federal court judgment in a habeas case is different than a direct appeal judgment. On a direct appeal judgment, the ﾂｭﾂｭ the court tells the district court, We're sending the case back to you, do A, B, C, and D or don't do X, Y, and Z. A Federal habeas judgment is a lawsuit against the warden having to do with the body of the prisoner. The Federal court doesn't have authority to tell the State to do or not do any particular thing. The effect of the Federal court judgment is to basically say release the prisoner unless within, in our case, 120 days you resentence him to life or you give a new punishment hearing.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Would your argument be the same if all of the claims in this case were not ineffective assistance of counsel claims? Let's take an example that's in the briefs ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ where there's ineffective assistance ﾂｭﾂｭ of ﾂｭﾂｭ there's a ineffective assistance of counsel claim and there's a coerced confession claim. And so the ﾂｭﾂｭ the petitioner wins on the ineffective assistance of counsel claim, loses in the district court on the coerced confession claim. The judgment is that he's entitled to resentencing or, let's say, it's resentencing due to the ineffective assistance of counsel, but there will be not be the opportunity to introduce ﾂｭﾂｭ to exclude the coerced confession. So would you ﾂｭﾂｭ does your argument apply in that situation as well?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Assuming the coerced confession involves the punishment phase as opposed to the guilt/innocence phase.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: The same phase, yes.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Okay. I think that's a situation where the State has a decent argument that because it's a different claim, that would perhaps entitle you to more relief under the judgment, that is, not just a new punishment hearing, but a new punishment hearing without the unconstitutional confession, that on the state of the law today, I would file a crossﾂｭappeal and move for a COA on a separate claim because I think that would be Pfeiffer, Alexander, El Paso Natural Gas, where you're seeking to modify the judgment and obtain more relief than it would be American Railway.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: If you say that ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Well, why do you ﾂｭﾂｭ go ahead. Sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, if I ﾂｭﾂｭ yeah, just a followﾂｭup on that. If you say that, could not the same situation arise with respect to different ineffective assistance of counsel claims? Let's say that in this case, you won on your Spisak claim, things that were said during the closing. You lost on the Wiggins claim. Let's say the Wiggins claim was that there was money available from the court ﾂｭﾂｭ from the State to hire experts, but the attorney didn't apply for the money, didn't do an investigation, that was ineffective assistance of counsel. So if you win on the ﾂｭﾂｭ on the Spisak claim, you'll ﾂｭﾂｭ you'll get a new sentencing hearing, presumably, free from the errors in the closing, but you won't get the opportunity to ﾂｭﾂｭ to go back and do the time or the money to ﾂｭﾂｭ to do the investigation.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I would respectfully disagree with that. Because if you get a new sentencing hearing, you get to do whatever you want to do at the new sentencing hearing. And for our purposes, if you now know for ﾂｭﾂｭ in Federal court that the lawyer didn't investigate mental history and discover X, Y, and Z, you now know it.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: But on ﾂｭﾂｭ on remand, they ﾂｭﾂｭ they say, well, that ﾂｭﾂｭ you lost that claim.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: No. It ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: You lost it, so there's no ﾂｭﾂｭ there's no need for more money. There's no need for more time. Let's do it over with what ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, with a ﾂｭﾂｭ with a proper closing.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: And that's the thing. The Federal habeas judgment cannot direct the State court what to do or not do at a retrial. All it can do is tell them we're releasing this person unless the constitutional error is fixed.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Well, that's not true. It ﾂｭﾂｭ it can tell them what to do. The ﾂｭﾂｭ the basis on which the conviction was set aside cannot be repeated by the State court. You're ﾂｭﾂｭ you're saying that the State court can ﾂｭﾂｭ is ﾂｭﾂｭ is free to make the same mistake again?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, it's actually not me saying. It's this Court's opinions have said that on a Federal habeas judgment does not decide what a State court may do or not do on remand. We just direct them to fix the constitutional error. We don't tell them how to do it.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But ﾂｭﾂｭ but ﾂｭﾂｭ but once you say we direct them to fix the constitutional error, that is something that they have to do on remand, isn't it?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Or ﾂｭﾂｭ or the Federal court will release the prisoner.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Fix that constitutional error.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But this other constitutional error, which we didn't rule on in the habeas thing, they don't have to fix. It's up to them.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: But let's remember, in an IAC claim, it's different than a freestanding admission or exclusion of evidence claim because a Federal court habeas judgment cannot ﾂｭﾂｭ for example, in this case, the State says, we sought to enlarge our rights under the judgment by asking the Federal court to order the State to fix any closing argument error and require a new trial free of this error. We didn't ask that. We could not have asked that. A Federal court cannot order a State court to fix any error of defense counsel or tell defense counsel how to try the case, what to offer, what not to offer, what arguments to make. All the Court can do is provide a remedy for the petitioner if he does not have effective counsel at the retrial.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: And that remedy is a new ﾂｭﾂｭ a new trial.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Or a new punishment.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What if ﾂｭﾂｭ what if ﾂｭﾂｭ what if one claim is the ineffective assistance and the other claim is a violation of the confrontation clause?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Separate.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: And you ﾂｭﾂｭ and you ﾂｭﾂｭ when ﾂｭﾂｭ when you go back, they can repeat the error or they can choose not to call their witness, either way. And it would seem to me that if you didn't file a crossﾂｭappeal, that the Federal court decision would be an advisory opinion on the confrontation clause.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: And so in that circumstance, if you were seeking additional relief, i.e., keep out the non ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ constitutional admission of evidence, then in that situation, I would file a crossﾂｭappeal and move for a COA because I would be seeking to enlarge my rights under the judgment. The judgment that we got here didn't really give us anything more than the Constitution gives any citizen the day that he walks into a courtroom, which is the right to a trial with the effective assistance of counsel.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: No, no, no, no. The ﾂｭﾂｭ the right to a trial that did not have this ﾂｭﾂｭ this failure of counsel, this particular failure. You're not entitled to ﾂｭﾂｭ to competent counsel. You're ﾂｭﾂｭ you're entitled to counsel who doesn't make a mistake. He could be the dumbest counsel around so long as he doesn't make a mistake. And he could be the smartest around, and if he does make a mistake, that's ineffective assistance of counsel.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Respectfully, it's not by itself. The mistakes may be mistakes, but unless the totality of those mistakes constitute sufficient prejudice to undermine confidence in the verdict ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: But was that an issue here? I mean, I ﾂｭﾂｭ I ﾂｭﾂｭ looking at the blue brief and the ﾂｭﾂｭ and the red brief, it seemed to me that the issues here have turned on a mistake, how many mistakes are there. It wasn't a question of prejudice. That is, the lower court said, even if you're ﾂｭﾂｭ if you're right ﾂｭﾂｭ and they thought you were right ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ that these two errors in failing to investigate the background created prejudice. So I don't think there was an argument on appeal about whether there was enough prejudice. It was a question of was this a mistake. Am I right or not?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, my position on appeal was I'm ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: No, I know. I'm just saying was there an argument about that? Did they disagree about that?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I think the State certainly disagreed with ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Did they ﾂｭﾂｭ and they argued that before the court.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: They ﾂｭﾂｭ they disagreed ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: They said even if their ﾂｭﾂｭ they said the issue before the ﾂｭﾂｭ the Fifth Circuit is whether all together these three things amount to prejudice because our view of the State's view was, all right, even if there were errors here, it didn't amount to prejudice?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: What they basically said is that the district court didn't defer to the State court findings and conclusions regarding those matters. So ﾂｭﾂｭ but let's ﾂｭﾂｭ let's play the State's argument out to its logical conclusion. Under their theory, let's assume the same thing happened to the district court. We win on A and B, we lose on C, and they say we're not going to appeal. Go back to State court, we'll give you a trial, and you can introduce the evidence of alleged mental deficits and disadvantaged background. We'll give you a chance to save your guy's life by laying it all out to a jury and letting them decide. And I say, nope, I'm going to appeal the closing argument to the circuit. And so I file a notice of appeal and move for a COA. And the district court ﾂｭﾂｭ let's see how that would play out. The district judge would say, did you read my opinion? You won. Why do you need a COA? Why do you want to take this up? And I would say, Judge, because I need an order for you to tell me that I cannot argue to a jury that I would agree with the death sentence. I need that order to keep me from doing that. And the judge would say: You are out of your mind; COA denied. I would appeal to the circuit. The State would take the exact opposite position they are taking here. They would say this is a frivolous appeal, he got all the relief he wanted in the judgment, which is a new punishment hearing, he doesn't have the right to appeal this, this is an example of death row inmates abusing the system and filing frivolous appeals to delay things.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: The predicate of the argument is that again that ineffective assistance of counsel claims are different. You wouldn't think that it was crazy in the situation where you've got the ineffective assistance of counsel claim and the coerced confession claim. You win on the ineffective assistance of counsel claim, they don't reach the coerced confession claim or the confrontation clause claim. It wouldn't be crazy for you to appeal the loss on either of those things because if you don't get relief on that then you are going to have the same thing when you go back, right?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: That's right. And in that situation I would be seeking to enlarge my rights under the judgment by having evidence excluded at a retrial that's beyond the scope of the judgment. In this case the judgment gave me everything I wanted. There is not a word in that judgment that's adverse to me. There's not a thing I could have asked for.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: The argument is that what the judgment was is the relief that you got is a new sentencing hearing shorn of the particular errors the district court found and those particular errors did not relate to the Spisak or closing argument. So you are trying to say we had a judgment, we won, and ordinarily a judgment winner can appeal if they won and if there is an argument that was made on which the judgment won or lost, that is not preclusive because he didn't have a chance to appeal. A judgment winner can't appeal. But this is peculiar because the ﾂｭﾂｭ there were ﾂｭﾂｭ the ﾂｭﾂｭ the order is you are entitled to a new trial shorn of these particular errors. The court of appeals says they are not errors. And then you want to bring up another error.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, I want to ﾂｭﾂｭ let's ﾂｭﾂｭ let's break down the way IAC is analyzed. The issue in this case was, was Petitioner ﾂｭﾂｭ denied the effective assistance of counsel at the punishment phase. In the vernacular, we made an IAC claim. We made ﾂｭﾂｭ an IAC claim, this Court said in Strickland, has two components: Deficient performance and prejudice. We made three arguments in support of deficient performance. We won two; we lost one. We won the claim. We got the new punishment hearing. So at the new punishment hearing ﾂｭﾂｭ let's assume we got one, and I was ﾂｭﾂｭ it doesn't mean I would have to put on the evidence of mental health history and disadvantaged background. I could make a strategic decision based on the landscape of the retrial what to use or not use. So the Federal court order would give the Petitioner a new punishment hearing, but it would not dictate to me what evidence I had to put on, what arguments I had to make, nor could the State or the trial court compel me to do or not do anything. It could only provide a remedy if I didn't do it correctly.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But if you fail to put on the same evidence which the court of appeals had said the failure to produce constituted ineffective performance of counsel, if you do the same thing again, why wouldn't be that ineffective assistance of counsel?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, it might or might not depending on how the case was retried. What if, for example, on the mental ﾂｭﾂｭ let's just take an example: The mental health history. Okay. The State went out and got a doctor to controvert the opinions of our doctors. What if I decided at the retrial, you know, I think that getting into this mental health history would create more harm than good based on what the State's doctor is going to say. I'm not going to put that on, I'm just going to go with the disadvantaged background and argue that they haven't proven future dangerousness because he's got no history of violence when he is incarcerated. I could make that strategic decision. If somebody wanted to come along later and grade my papers, they could, and they could say it was thumbs up or thumbs down as the case may be. Let's examine the State's argument ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Now, you couldn't put ﾂｭﾂｭ if all things otherwise were the same, you couldn't do what the lawyer did here without being ineffective assistance. That's what ﾂｭﾂｭ isn't that their holding, the holding of the district court? The district court says the lawyer in these circumstances did not give effective assistance as to two things. So of course things could change in the new trial, and then you could act differently, but if they didn't change, you would have to do ﾂｭﾂｭ act ﾂｭﾂｭ what the district court said, wouldn't you?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I would agree with regard to the evidence of disadvantaged background, because that is written in stone. That doesn't change.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right. Now, once that is so, there is nothing in the order that would, things being identical, stop the prosecutor from making precisely the same remarks that you are challenging.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, but see, it wasn't the prosecutor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Is that ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: It wasn't the prosecutor that made the argument. It was the defense ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right, fine. Whatever the third error is ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ there is nothing in the order that would prevent the lawyer from doing precisely the same thing. There is something in the order that would prevent you from doing the same first two things, other things being precisely equal.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: If I may, let's look at the mental health history for a moment. The mental health history was not even known by the trial lawyer. Okay? So if he was ineffective, he was ineffective based on a Wiggins failure to investigate type of situation. That's a different question. Let's assume he had investigated and he found the same things that I found during habeas, and he decided not to put it on. Then it would be a strategic decision which he would have to defend. And so that's where we come back, because that evidence was never known ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But why are we talking about the claim on which you lost on appeal? You are not going to get a chance to go back with disadvantaged, with mental health, because you lost on those on appeal.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I don't think ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: I thought the Wiggins claims were rejected on appeal.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: They were. But let's play this ﾂｭﾂｭ let's assume that we get merits review on the closing argument. And remember, it's not a claim, in my view. It's an argument in support of a claim. And I think that the key portion of the government's ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: May I just clarify it?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Everything that you are arguing rides on our treating ineffective assistance of counsel as one claim rather than ineffective assistance of counsel, confrontation clause, some other violation. If we don't buy your argument that ineffective assistance of counsel is one monolithic claim and we think the division between what they ﾂｭﾂｭ what they're calling Wiggins error and the ﾂｭﾂｭ those ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: The two Spisak claims.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ you would lose.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I disagree. I don't think you even need to reach the issue of is IAC one claim or not. It's here because they've split my claim into thirds by saying I made three claims instead of one. I can still win under a pure interpretation of American Railway because I am not seeking greater relief than what I got in the judgment, which is a new punishment hearing.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Would ﾂｭﾂｭ would you agree that to include all of the counsel's errors it's important to show the substantiality of the error?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: That's what I did in the Fifth Circuit, was say, look ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: All right. Would you agree that that goes to show how substantial the incompetence was?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: The combined effect relates ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ to the prejudice ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ inquiry, but of course one alone can be sufficiently prejudicial.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: With that in mind, I want to turn to the COA statute.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Let's assume that we accept your argument that you did not have to file a separate notice of appeal.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Does that mean that you automatically comply with the COA? Because the COA statute says that a certificate of appeal ﾂｭﾂｭ appealability must show that the applicant has made a substantial showing. And you've conceded that all aspects of the incompetence of counsel go to substantiality, so why is it that you have complied with the COA statute?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, I haven't complied ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: And the under ﾂｭﾂｭ and the overlying question is once you ﾂｭﾂｭ if you were to convince us that no notice of appeal was required, does it automatically follow that you are not required to have a COA?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I think that's the key issue. The rule I propose, that I think is based on the statute and the ﾂｭﾂｭ and the text of the rules, is if you need to crossﾂｭappeal, you need a COA. If you do not need to crossﾂｭappeal, you don't need a COA. And let me give you two ﾂｭﾂｭ let me hit the government's arguments headﾂｭon, and I think this is key. They say the COA requirement should be read into the statute or adopted as a matter of Federal common law. That is a tacit acknowledgment that the statute does not require a COA for the nonﾂｭappealing petitioner appellee. In their brief, they do not rely on the text of 2253(c) or the associated rules, FRAP 22(b), or Habeas Rule 11(a). They want you all to ﾂｭﾂｭ the Court to read that into the statute. And that is not appropriate where the text is clear, where Congress ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Are you saying that the COA statute itself says you need a COA to appeal from the final order and you don't want to appeal from the final order because you like the final order?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, correct ﾂｭﾂｭ in part correct. 2253(c) says a COA is required for an appeal to be taken to the court of appeals. Now, we know that doesn't apply to the government for two reasons. First, under (c)(2) a governmental entity could never show the denial of a Federal constitutional right. Secondly, under FRAP 22(b)(3) the government is expressly excluded. So if it doesn't apply to the government, who does it apply to? Who did Congress intend for 2253(c) to apply to? The answer is clear. Petitionerﾂｭappellants who take the appeal, or petitionerﾂｭappellees who take a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: So why wouldn't you have to take a crossﾂｭappeal?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Because, well, I ﾂｭﾂｭ that gets us back to where we were ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: It's the same problem, isn't it? I mean, do you help yourself by saying, okay, we need to crossﾂｭappeal, it only applies there, because then we get into exactly the same argument, whether you need a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, I'd say I don't need to crossﾂｭappeal because I'm not seeking to enlarge my rights in the judgment.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Well, yes, you are, because then ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ and IAC is a single claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ we're back to the same argument. What?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: IAC is a single claim, and I ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Let's assume that we disagree with you that you needed a crossﾂｭappeal. Could you answer or didn't ﾂｭﾂｭ or agree with you, you didn't need a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Answer Justice Kennedy's question of why you don't need the COA.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because a crossﾂｭappeal doesn't have to make a substantial showing of ﾂｭﾂｭ of a denial of a constitutional right.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: A crossﾂｭappeal is just a piece of paper that you file. It doesn't have to show anything. It just says I want to appeal. It's the COA ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Exactly. So why are the two tied together, in your mind?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well, because I think when you read 2253(c) it's clear it applies only to the petitioner appellant who lost, obviously, on everything below or the petitioner appellee who lost on a separate claim that he's now desiring to appeal. And ﾂｭﾂｭ and this is kind of, I think, obvious to me if you look at habeas Rule 11(a), which says that, "The district court must issue or deny a COA when it enters a final order adverse to the applicant." The applicant is the party taking the appeal. Here the district court did not enter a final order adverse to me, and I didn't take the appeal. So you don't reach that part of 11(a) that says if a COA is required, it has to specify the issue or issues that satisfy the showing of the denial of the Federal constitutional right.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Do you agree that a petitioner who has to take a crossﾂｭappeal, because the petitioner wants to enlarge the judgment, does not have to get a certificate of appealability?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Do I agree that he does or does not?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Does not.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: He has to.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I think if you take a crossﾂｭappeal, you need a COA. And here's ﾂｭﾂｭ here's the other thing and I do want to hit this. The government ﾂｭﾂｭ the State says the COA should be required as a matter of policy to screen frivolous appeals, and indeed, that's the most attractive argument they make because what court doesn't want to screen frivolous appeals. I want you to bear in mind, please, that there's only a few cases each year in which the petitioner prevails in the district court under the daunting AEDPA standard of review and the State appeals, the information we received off of the public databases that we were able to check. And so I put an asterisk next to this, is that in 2013, there were 18 cases in the Federal circuits that have our scenario where the petitioner won and the State took the appeal. So that's less than 2 per ﾂｭﾂｭ per circuit. That's a speck of sand on the beach of cases. Where a petitioner prevails in the district court under the AEDPA standard of review, that appeal is not a frivolous appeal. The appellate court is going to have to spend its time on it. It's serious, especially in a death penalty case. And the briefing rules, the page limits, the fact that the petitioner would have an incentive to protect his judgment and focus his efforts on the claim he won would certainly discourage people from doing what the government says, which is to raise 100 frivolous arguments in response. If that were to happen in this speck of sand number of cases in the appellate courts, I'm totally confident that the circuit courts can dispose of frivolous arguments in a sentence, a footnote or a paragraph like that. Before I reserve the rest of my time for rebuttal, I want you ﾂｭﾂｭ I don't know whether this Court appreciates irony, but I suspect that you do, and I consider it ironic that the State is basically trying to scare this Court into reading a COA requirement into the statute for a petitioner appellee under the guise of the appellate courts being inundated with 100 frivolous arguments by prevailing petitioners in a case where the prevailing petitioner raised exactly one argument, and it comprised 3 pages out of a 53ﾂｭpage brief in which the first 50 pages sought to protect the judgment. A final thought. If this Court were to rule in our favor, I don't see there being any seismic shift in the Federal habeas landscape. This case, in the great scheme of things, is a blip on the Federal habeas radar. But if you accept the State's position and rule in their favor, in my judgment, the Court would have to find that ineffective assistance of counsel constitutes separate claims based on the number of allegations of deficient performance and that cannot be harmonized with Strickland. Under the State's theory, there would be no circumstances, there would be no case in which the petitioner appellee did not need to crossﾂｭappeal and obtain the COA. Viewing this in context and boiling it down to the bottom line, I filed one IAC claim. I had one child, and I named him Strickland. The State cannot come back now and tell you that I had triplets and their names are Wiggins I, Wiggins II and Spisak.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: In other contexts, however, IAC is not a unitary concept, is it? I don't want to take up your rebuttal time, but in other contexts like repetitive litigation or exhaustion, IAC is not a unitary concept.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Well ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Talk about it on rebuttal if you ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I don't want to get too far into that, but it's a totally different situation there, because those cases, Trevino and so forth, they're based on principles ﾂｭﾂｭ principles of federalism and comity. It's not a lack of jurisdiction. It's that the Court can decide to excuse some procedural default based on cause and prejudice. In our case, it's a pure question of appellate court jurisdiction. Does the court of appeals have jurisdiction to rule on an argument in support of ineffective assistance where I've already won on the claim? All I'm asking them to do is consider the argument as an alternate basis to affirm the judgment of the district court. And for that reason, this Court should reverse the judgment of the Fifth Circuit and remand for consideration of the closing argument on the merits. Thank you. I will save the rest of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counselor. Mr. Oldham.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: It's now common ground that Petitioner would need to crossﾂｭappeal to add some conditions to a conditional release order issued by the district court. The only question in this case now is whether the particular condition requested by this particular Petitioner was necessary or sufficient to add to the conditional release order, and we think that it was. We think that this particular condition, the Spisak error, was a meaningful condition and it necessitated a crossﾂｭappeal for two reasons. First, if Petitioner had crossﾂｭappealed and prevailed on the Spisak claim, the State would have been obligated to fix that error, to cure that error upon pain of immediate release of the prisoner. And that is a dramatically form ﾂｭﾂｭ dramatically expanded form of relief that would necessitate a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: How would you do that? How would you cure the Spisak error?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, there are many ways that the State and the defense and ﾂｭﾂｭ I'm sorry, I should say the prosecution and the defense and the State court could cure the Spisak error. It starts with the enunciation of the standard from the Federal district court as to exactly what the Sixth Amendment requires of a closing argument, which then guides both the ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, the Spisak error is something that the lawyer said in closing argument. You're going to have a new trial, right? Well, presumably, I mean, the lawyer may say the same thing or the new lawyer may say the exact same thing or he may not. It seems to me that it's a fairly academic dispute as to whether or not you have a separate claim once you have the relief of a new trial.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, we don't think it's an academic claim with respect to adding the condition that requires a certain level of constitutional performance for the defense counsel ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: There's always ﾂｭﾂｭ you always have the same level of constitutional performance. This is only going to come up as a particular problem if for some reason the lawyer says exactly the same thing in closing as the prior lawyer did. And I don't anticipate that happening.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, this actually happens in ineffective assistance of counsel claims in other contexts where the State court has the power, for example, to conduct a colloquy with the defense lawyer to probe what the judgment is that has informed the defense lawyer's representation, what the defense lawyer has done, what the defense lawyer plans to do. And, of course, the State has the ability to object and move for curative instructions if the error recurs. But the fundamental ﾂｭﾂｭ the really important point is that if the error recurs or if the State fails to fix it, the remedy is immediate release, which we should all agree is a dramatically expanded form of ﾂｭﾂｭ of relief, which would necessitate a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Oldham, some of my questions to ﾂｭﾂｭ to your opposing counsel suggest that ineffective assistance of counsel is mistake by mistake. That there are here three claims of ineffective assistance, not just one. Maybe that's so. What do you do with this case where the ﾂｭﾂｭ the trial court finds two instances of ineffective assistance, the State agrees that those two are valid. But appeals on the ground that it was harmless error, that the cumulative effect of those two mistakes was not enough to change the outcome of the trial. The defendant, on the other hand, wants to raise the issue that there was, indeed, a third instance of ineffective assistance, and then if you combined all three, there would have been sufficient harm to enable setting aside the verdict. What do you do with that case? There ﾂｭﾂｭ there the three separate instances have sort of been combined into one where ﾂｭﾂｭ where the issue is the harmful effect.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: The answer, Justice Scalia, would turn on the particularities of the third error of the hypothetical. That is, if the third error was sufficient to push it across the line of the Sixth Amendment in the deficient performance standard, then that would be the contours of the ineffective assistance of counsel claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Okay. So you would allow that one to be ﾂｭﾂｭ to be raised without a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: But it would ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: So long as it ﾂｭﾂｭ it would ﾂｭﾂｭ or at least so long as it's argued that that would produce the incremental effect of reversing the judgment.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Yes. We agree with the Clarence Darrow hypothetical from the top half of the argument, which is that you measure the constitutional error based on where the lawyer's performance transgresses the balance of the Sixth Amendment. And so if in the hypothetical, if that happened to have been on the third error, then that would be the contours of the ineffectiveness claim. To return to the Chief Justice's ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Before you leave that, I thought that your opposing counsel said that did happen here. It was ﾂｭﾂｭ you did raise a question of ﾂｭﾂｭ of whether there was enough prejudice and, therefore, they were entitled to bring in the third as showing that even if the first two weren't sufficient, the third made up for it.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Justice Breyer, we disagree with that characterization of what happened in the court below. What happened in the court below and in the District Court was that petitioner raised three allegations of deficiency. This is how he characterized it in his own certiorari petition to this Court. Three allegations of deficiency, each of which standing alone both transgressed the bounds of the Sixth Amendment and was a prejudicial error. We fought back on two because the District Court had granted two, and those were reversed in the Fifth Circuit. He raised a third independent error and if he wants to add that third independent error to the conditional release order, that is he wants the State to be held responsible and a constitutional error imputed to the State sufficient to command his immediate release upon failing to fix it at the second trial ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: The first two were thrown out on the grounds that there was no error; right?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Not on the ground that the effect was not harmful enough to warrant setting aside the judgment.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: That's correct. They were under ﾂｭﾂｭ that's correct. And although the State did brief it ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, what do you do with the COA? I mean, however we rule, we may be creating headaches. Okay. So petitioner wins on two and he loses on the third issue, the ﾂｭﾂｭ the closing summation. Does he have to then go for the COA? He can't wait for you to appeal and then make that decision, can he?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, under our view and under the hypothetical given on the top side of the argument he could crossﾂｭappeal and that actually ﾂｭﾂｭ and of course would have to get a COA. But this is actually ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Crossﾂｭappeal when there's no appeal?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: He could have ﾂｭﾂｭ he could have taken the first appeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: He could not have taken an appeal. Only the State could take an appeal; right?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Our view is that on this particular judgment he could have taken the first appeal. And that's incredibly important because it would give him an additional protection if there was a second trial. In particular, if he could get a Federal court to say the Sixth Amendment demands this level of competence ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Where does the statute or the rule talk about somebody who loses on a particular claim but wins the judgment, I thought ﾂｭﾂｭ I'm looking at the order. The order is on page 33 of the appendix and it doesn't say anything about Spisak or about ﾂｭﾂｭ what was the other one ﾂｭﾂｭ Wiggins. It just says the order is three choices, release him from custody or give him a new sentencing hearing or sentence him to a term in prison. That's it. That's the order that we窶决e reviewing ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Ginsburg, but it must be read in the context of the entire opinion. As we pointed out in footnote 6 of the red brief, this Court and other Federal courts often write their conditional release orders differently. To say, for example, as Judge Pollack did in the Lamb case, these particular errors have to be fixed. And everyone seems to agree that if the judgment said these particular errors have to be fixed, he could have taken the first appeal or he could take a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: If he could take his first appeal, how does it work? That is, it seems to me that I've seen lots of petitions in habeas where an incarcerated person will bring up 40 arguments. Now, suppose that he loses on 39 and he wins on one and he gets his new trial, and the court ﾂｭﾂｭ the prosecution says fine, we'll go ahead, we'll give you the new trial. Is the lawyer then supposed to appeal his 39 losses?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, as a practical matter, the notice of appeal deadline would be far in advance of the retrial decision.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: No, no. You said he could take the first appeal. So before the new trial goes on he says, I think I'll take the first appeal on the other 39 that I lost on. Can he do that?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Absolutely. And for ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Well, then a lot of people, you know, I'm not saying they're badly motivated, they're in prison, they might think this sort of interesting, I might lose at trial, let's just see if I can't find a few other grounds here.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, it's obviously not a lot of people. It's ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I've never heard of that. Have you found examples where that's happened?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, as the petitioner points out, there's only 18 of these cases in the Court of Appeals from last year, but a great example ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Wait. Have you found many, one, cases where a petitioner won in the District Court, there is no State appeal, but he did lose on other grounds and he decided to take a first appeal on several of those other grounds?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: I'm unaware of any but I ﾂｭﾂｭ only because the State often appeals ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, the reason there might not be any, I mean, any decision by the Federal court in that situation would be, I think, a purely advisory opinion. Let's say he loses on a confrontation clause claim, he wins on inadequate assistance of counsel. He takes the appeal. If I'm the judge on appeal I say, well, in the new trial, they may not call the witness that you said shouldn't have been called and you want me to issue an advisory opinion just in case they do. I don't see how he can do that.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, it wouldn't be an advisory opinion in this sense. It would say that what happened in the previous trial transgressed the confrontation clause or the Sixth Amendment, depending on which case ﾂｭﾂｭ which claimant lost below and it would give him a very valuable procedural protection at the second trial.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: No. It's superfluous in the sense that it is unnecessary to the judgment. He got the judgment, he won it. The trial was set aside. The conviction was set aside, he got a new trial. And he's saying, oh, there are additional reasons why I should have gotten a new trial. I'm not going to listen to that. That's absurd. You got what you wanted. Now go away.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: That ﾂｭﾂｭ Justice Scalia, our view of the conditional release order is significantly weightier than that. What we think a conditional release order is and what we think that this Court's habeas cases demand is that a conditional release order is a Federal court's order on a constitutional claim that says the State must release, that is the actual quintessential habeas remedy, or fix these errors. Never does the conditional release order say, you get a new trial.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But let's ﾂｭﾂｭ you may be ﾂｭﾂｭ this one did, said release ﾂｭﾂｭ give him a new hearing or release him. But窶祢窶冦 takingﾂｭﾂｭ I do want you to answer my COA question, okay, because what you're saying is exactly what Justice Scalia is worried about, which is, is he going to go present the COA and are we going to even entertain it since he won his release? But if he ﾂｭﾂｭ does he have to get a COA? But more importantly, what happens when there's a new hearing? Those two mistakes are not repeated but a new one is introduced and the old one is repeated. Are you saying he can't appeal again?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: On the old one or the new one?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Our rule is actually much more ﾂｭﾂｭ much more protective of the prisoner than that. Because if the old error is recommitted he gets immediate release. And that's the ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, that's ﾂｭﾂｭ you're saying he only gets immediate release if it's the old error that the judge found on the first habeas.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But let's assume that the second error is ﾂｭﾂｭ the third error is committed and even a new one is committed that wasn't a part of the first function, can he appeal?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Of course. Of course. And he has ﾂｭﾂｭ yes, he has a new judgment. He can challenge the new judgment. The only question is how much relief affords to him. Because he agrees with us, to go back to Justice Scalia's example, he agrees with us that he could ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Can we go back to basics. Isn't it true that a judgment winner does not have to appeal an issue on which he lost but he is ﾂｭﾂｭ if he's content with the judgment, he can present arguments that would entitle him to relief. He can assert defensively. A judgment winner doesn't have to appeal, doesn't have to crossﾂｭappeal. That is the general rule; right?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Yes, we agree. The only question is whether he's a judgment winner and we pointed out many examples where people can be prevailing parties in the sense that the judgment imposes no liability on them but they can nonetheless appeal and we would submit that this is one of them, and it's precisely because he wants the additional protection. To go back to Justice Scalia's example, everyone agrees that if the petitioner wins on one claim but he wants to raise a coerced confession claim he can file a first appeal or a crossﾂｭappeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Oldham, suppose it were he just had two Wiggins claims. One was the lawyer didn't find the psychological report and the other was the lawyer failed to put on my mother as a character witness. He loses one, he wins the other. Does he have to take a crossﾂｭappeal on that?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: He has to take a crossﾂｭappeal if he wants the one he lost on added to the conditional release order. That is, he wants the protection that the Federal court would provide against that error being either recommitted or failing ﾂｭﾂｭ the State failing to fix it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: But they ﾂｭﾂｭ you keep talking about the conditional release order. It doesn't say anything about what the State must do, what errors it must correct, it's on page 35, it just says give him a new sentencing hearing or sentence him to a term of imprisonment. It doesn't say, because of this, because of that, because of this.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: And that's absolutely correct. But we ﾂｭﾂｭ it seems like both sides agree that it could have said exactly what the opinion says. That is, I found two errors here, I have not found an error on the third ground and therefore, it could have been incorporated in the text, as they often do. As I say, footnote 6 of the red brief collects a series of the ways that Federal courts phrase them, and it would be a bizarre crossﾂｭappeal that would turn on the particular phraseology of the conditional release order when everyone agrees that the meaning of it is the same.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Let ﾂｭﾂｭ let me just ask you this question about COAs. Let's just take a case ﾂｭﾂｭ there's no crossﾂｭappeal or anything. The prisoner alleges in ﾂｭﾂｭ inadequate assistance of counsel, and he loses. He files a COA. He says, my counsel was inadequate because there were two Wiggins errors, he did not ﾂｭﾂｭ two Wiggins errors. That is what he says in the COA. The COA is granted. Now he files his brief. Can he allege a Spisak error as well?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: As far as I know, no court in the United States ﾂｭﾂｭ no circuit court in the United States would allow him to do that because of 2253(c)(3).

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: So ﾂｭﾂｭ so can he ﾂｭﾂｭ so in other words, in a COA, you have to list every error that counsel made and at ﾂｭﾂｭ at ﾂｭﾂｭ on ﾂｭﾂｭ on pain of not being able to argue that in your brief?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. That is what 2253(c)(3) says, issueﾂｭbyﾂｭissue specification. And every court of appeals in the United States agrees, even the Seventh Circuit, that would seem to ﾂｭﾂｭ would say all three of the errors that you hypothesize would be the same. Even in the Seventh Circuit, you would have to get a certificate of appealability in that circumstance.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But that's ﾂｭﾂｭ (c)(3) comes after (c)(1). And (c)(1) says you need a COA to appeal from the final order. And so if you don't need a COA to appeal from the final order, you go on and appeal from the final order. The final order is fine. You would never get to (c)(3). (C)(3) says if you need a COA, then you will indicate which specific issues. But it doesn't ﾂｭﾂｭ tells you that.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Ginsburg. And (c)(1), as it's currently written, predated even AEDPA. And even before AEDPA, that is in 1986, the Second Circuit interpreted (c)(1) as it is currently written to require prisoners, when they are the appellees, to nonetheless get COAs. And as we sit here today, 6 out the 8 ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Where does that ﾂｭﾂｭ where does that come from in the statute? I mean, the statute says you need the COA to appeal from the final order in a habeas proceeding. Well ﾂｭﾂｭ in the final order. I don't want an appeal from the final order. It's good.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: So the preﾂｭAEDPA standard was the certificate of probable cause standard, which courts of appeal, starting with the Roman decision in 1986, required prisoners to satisfy the issue ﾂｭﾂｭ the ﾂｭﾂｭ the CPC requirement even as the appellee. And as we sit here today, 6 out of the 8 courts of appeals would require the Petitioner in this case to get a certificate of appealability.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right. Suppose the ﾂｭﾂｭ isn't it ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: In fact, you get it from the language of the statute, which says you need a certificate to appeal from the final order.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, Justice Ginsburg, as this Court has said twice in both the MillerﾂｭEl and in Slack, the language of this statute, 2253(c), imposes a list of necessary but not sufficient conditions. And it is entirely within the provenance of the courts of appeals, to deal with these issues on a regular basis, to interpret that language to also apply to a prisoner who is the appellee, who wants to raise an issue for the court of appeal's consideration, both because that's the purpose of the statute and because it was the preﾂｭAEDPA standard upon which the statute was enacted. And so if I might just return to the Chief Justice's hypothetical about the potential for something not happening again. This is ﾂｭﾂｭ it's much more significant than simply that. If you think about the quintessential error that could never recur, it would be, for example, a Brady violation, that is, the withholding of exculpatory evidence. Once it's in the possession of the defendant, how could it ever recur? But it's in precisely that circumstance that one of the most powerful exhibitions of a conditional release order has worked out because in the ﾂｭﾂｭ in the Wolf v. Clark case, the Fourth Circuit, the Commonwealth of Virginia received a Brady remedy, that is, it ﾂｭﾂｭ it had its conviction vacated because the ﾂｭﾂｭ the particular evidence had not been turned over to the defense. Now, that error, that particular error could never be recommitted, but the Commonwealth violated the spirit and the intention of that conditional release order by interviewing the jailhouse informant that gave the evidence, threatening him with perjury, and convincing him not to testify at the second trial. And in that circumstance, because the conditional release order ﾂｭﾂｭ which was phrased materially identically to ours, by the way ﾂｭﾂｭ the phraseology did not mention Brady, but because he had the power of a Federal conditional release order, instead of having to sit through another trial, instead of having to have the Brady evidence excluded, he was able to go back to the Federal district court that issued the original conditional order and ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Oh, sure. I mean, there may be situations in which the State or the ineffective counsel, they do repeat the error, or they may not.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, this one couldn't have been repeated. It was, by definition, not repeatable. But it was because the State failed to fix it. See, it's actually a significantly greater obligation on the State than not ﾂｭﾂｭ than simply not doing the same thing over again. Right?

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Thinking here to the ﾂｭﾂｭ the Criminal Justice Foundation filed a brief, and ﾂｭﾂｭ and as I read it, I thought, well, the point ﾂｭﾂｭ the point of this is don't make it too complicated. So they say, well, let's just consider what Estelle says, you Can窶冲 be deprived ofﾂｭﾂｭ you have to make a substantial showing of the denial of a Federal right. So they're saying the Federal right here that we're deprived of is a Sixth Amendment right to ﾂｭﾂｭ to counsel, period. There are a lot of different bases for it. There are a lot of different things that happen, but that's the single ground or right or, in this case, issue doesn't change the word of ﾂｭﾂｭ that it used to mean. So call it this one thing. It's so simple, and at that point, let the court of appeals deal with it. As soon as we try to separate different bases for saying that this was a deprivation of the right to counsel, we are going to produce a pretty good nightmare because they shade into each other often. They're related in a variety of ways. They may be or may not be related through the prejudice problem. And lawyers will start wondering when they have to file a court crossﾂｭappeal, and we will create a ﾂｭﾂｭ a mess. So that's the basic argument, I think, of this brief, and it is one of the arguments that they're trying to make. Now, what is your simple, clear answer to that?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: The current rule in 6 out of the 8 Courts of Appeals is not a nightmare. And the current rule reads issue in 2253(c)(3) to mean issue, not to mean a single claim. And so the nightmare that's predicted or the ﾂｭﾂｭ the pragmatic solution isn't in want of a problem in the sense that this is the ﾂｭﾂｭ the rule that we have advanced on the certificate of appealability is the majority rule. And as far as ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But isn't that a rule that was set up, as Justice Ginsburg said, only when the petitioner hasn't won. But here, there's a judgment for resentencing. As the Chief Justice indicates, the error may or may not ﾂｭﾂｭ the third error may or may not be repeated. So go back to that point, which is if you're a successful litigant, do you even have a right to try to go get a COA?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: So the rule that I'm providing, the 6 out of 8, is in the circumstance where the prisoner won and wants to, nonetheless, raise additional issues. So it's exactly this case. And the simple fact of recurrence is both a ﾂｭﾂｭ a difficult rule to predict exﾂｭante as the Wolf case would illustrate, but it's also ﾂｭﾂｭ would give short shrift to ineffective assistance of counsel claims because ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Why doesn't this fit ﾂｭﾂｭ why doesn't this case just fit what is the standard rule that a party who's content with the judgment doesn't have to crossﾂｭappeal to preserve arguments that enabled him to retain the benefits of the judgment? That's the standard rule.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, it fits within that rule. It just begs the question of what the judgment is. And in our ﾂｭﾂｭ in our view, the judgment entitles him to ﾂｭﾂｭ to ﾂｭﾂｭ to either release or to a resentencing under particular terms. And if he wants to change the terms of the resentencing, he's attacking the judgment and expanding his relief. We all agree that he would be attacking the judgment and expanding his relief if he wanted to add coerced confession. Our view is that ineffective assistance of counsel is not a second class right. It is not one that can be subordinated to a coerced confession claim, and that for the exact same reasons that the coerced confession claim may come up, may not come up, may be difficult to understand exﾂｭante, the practical significance of it may be difficult to discern exﾂｭante, he still has to crossﾂｭappeal in both circumstances. Yes, he conceded in the top ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But if I understand the implications ﾂｭﾂｭ this goes back to, I guess, the hypothetical I asked before. Now I have 5 Wiggins claims. You didn't put on my mother, and you didn't put on my brother, and you didn't put on my sister, you didn't put on my uncle, you didn't put on my aunt, and the court says, you know, we think your brother and your mother really did have something to say, we're not so sure about the others. Do you think I have to crossﾂｭappeal on my aunt and my sister and my uncle, and not only did you think I have to crossﾂｭappeal, but you think that if I had won and the State had not taken an appeal, I would have had to appeal myself?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, two things, Justice Kagan. One is that on ﾂｭﾂｭ on that particular hypothetical, I would suggest that there's just one Wiggins claim, and that is failure to call family to testify. And so there is sort of a tension in how we conceive of the claim in this case and it's ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Well, you know, I could do another hypothetical ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ failure to call family, failure to call the psychiatrist, failure to find the psychiatric report ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ failure to go investigate my mental background.

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Absolutely. And our position is, yes, it's not that you have to crossﾂｭappeal, it's that you can crossﾂｭappeal, right, in the sense that you could, if you wanted to go back and have a second trial without having the ones that you lost protected by the Federal Constitution, then you can. But if you want to have a conditional release order that commands the State to fix those particular errors upon pain of immediately releasing you, our rule allows you to crossﾂｭappeal, whereas their rule does not.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So do you need a COA, too?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Yes. Yes, Your Honor. You would always need a certificate of appealability.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: How do you do that? If ﾂｭﾂｭ take Justice Kagan's example. You've won, and you want to ﾂｭﾂｭ you're ﾂｭﾂｭ you're willing to forego unless there's an appeal by you. Now I'm going to crossﾂｭappeal. Now I need a COA, too, before I know what you're doing?

turns.name: MR. OLDHAM
turns.text: Well, the prisoner ﾂｭﾂｭ it's common ground that if the prisoner appeals on the top or the bottom the prisoner needs a certificate of appealability. And our position is that the particular rules that we're offering here offer protection to prisoners in the sense that ﾂｭﾂｭ and it offers protections to the State as well, right? It lets everyone know before we have to go back and have another trial about the mother or the sister or the cousin or the psychiatric report what the rules of the Sixth Amendment game are. It allows the prisoner to know that if the State doesn't fix that error he gets to go free and it allows the State to make a decision as to perhaps not even conducting a trial again. We've offered this example in the briefs, it's been ﾂｭﾂｭ and it's unrebutted in the reply that depending on what the particular conditions are, the State may forego and commute to a term of years, which is the ultimate benefit to the prisoner. And so it's incredibly important to give a prisoner the right to crossﾂｭappeal in that circumstance. And if he wants the court of appeals to address that issue he has to crossﾂｭappeal because it would be expanding that relief by adding a condition on a conditional release order that the State must obey or fix or otherwise let him go immediately. If there are no further questions.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Schaffer, two minutes left.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I'm a ﾂｭﾂｭ as the Court can probably tell, I'm a fairly simpleﾂｭminded person, and when I looked at this and said is there anything I need to do to get this closing argument in front of the circuit in response to the State's appeal, I said: Well, no; I won on ineffective assistance of counsel. I'm just making another argument in support of the judgment as to why there was prejudice, end of story. That's the extent of my thought. The bulk of these Federal habeas cases, as I'm sure the Court knows, 90 percent or more are pro se State court prisoners trying to navigate the treacherous AEDPA waters without any help at all. And it's tough enough to win on the merits. To create the procedural nightmare of even getting into the ballpark that the State would have you create is like throwing out the baby with the bath water. The simple thing is we want to make it easy for the courts to acquire jurisdiction.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The only way you can win this, according to you, is if we rule IAC claims are one claim?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: No. I can also win, Justice Sotomayor, on the basis that, even if IAC is multiple claims, I did not seek to enlarge my rights under the judgment. And it goes back to the State's position ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So when is it that you have to crossﾂｭappeal?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: If I want to enlarge my rights. For example, if I win on punishment phase IAC and I lose on guilt phase whatever and I want more than a new punishment hearing, I would have to crossﾂｭappeal obviously in that situation.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Well, you answered no, but then your explanation produces yes. You're saying you don't have to crossﾂｭappeal because you won on an ineffective assistance of counsel claim; right? There is one, you know, omnibus ineffective assistance of counsel claim. You won on that; right?

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: So your answer to Justice Sotomayor should have been the opposite of what it was. If you said yes, you should have said no. If you said no, you should of said yes.

turns.name: MR. SCHAFFER
turns.text: I do that all the time. But my understanding was she was asking me when would you need to crossﾂｭappeal, and I was saying the scenario in which you would. This is not one of them. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
